exemplar.title: To what extent have foreign investments in Torontoâs Housing Market affected the housing prices and affordability?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent have foreign investments in Torontoâs Housing Market affected the housing prices and affordability?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and directly frames the entire investigation
Topic is communicated accurately and effectively throughout the essay with sustained focus
Appropriate scope and clear sub-section headings ensure each part of the essay ties back to the central question
weaknesses: Methodology section lacks explicit detail on analytical tools (e.g., regression models, elasticity calculations)
No clear strategy to mitigate or triangulate data bias from TRREB or media sources
Selection criteria for secondary sources (news reports, journals) are not systematically justified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay excels at clearly defining and communicating its purpose and research question, sustaining a tight focus on foreign investmentâs impact on Toronto housing. There is evidence of informed source selection, but the methodology is only partially justified and lacks full transparency on analytic procedures and bias-Â­mitigation strategies. This places the work solidly in the 5â6 band for topic clarity and focus but at the top of the 3â4 band for methodology completeness. On balance, a score of 5 accurately reflects very strong focus and purpose with some methodological shortcomings.

strengths: Demonstrates a clear understanding of core economic concepts (demand, supply, elasticity) and applies them to the Toronto housing context
Uses national statistics and government releases appropriately to support tables and graphs
Terminology (e.g., âinelastic supplyâ, âdeadweight lossâ) is generally accurate and consistently employed
weaknesses: Over-reliance on real-estate blogs and media articles reduces academic rigor and source relevance
Key definitions (e.g., âforeign investorâ) lack precise legal citation, weakening disciplinary accuracy
Several tables and figures lack full source attribution, units or consistent citation, impairing traceability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows a solid, though at times descriptive, grasp of economic theory and applies relevant source material in most sections. However, inconsistent source rigor, occasional concept mis-definitions, and missing attributions prevent a higher band placement. A score of 3 is appropriate, reflecting overall clarity of knowledge tempered by weaknesses in source legitimacy and disciplinary precision.

strengths: Majority of research is clearly relevant to the research question, drawing on credible national and policy data
Arguments link data-driven analysis to economic theory, moving beyond pure description
Reasoned discussion of policy measures (Foreign Buyerâs Tax, Vacant Home Tax) demonstrates analytical engagement
weaknesses: Analysis lacks quantitative depth: no computed elasticity coefficients or econometric testing to support conclusions
Economic models are sometimes mis-applied (e.g., incorrect tax shift in supplyâdemand diagram)
Critical evaluation is limited, missing systematic consideration of alternative explanations and policy trade-offs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay makes effective use of relevant research and constructs a logical argument linking foreign investment to price effects. However, the depth of analysis is modest, with little original quantitative work and some conceptual errors in modeling. Evaluation of policy is thoughtful but not fully critical. These factors align with the lower end of the 4â6 band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Overall structure follows EE conventions (introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion) with a contents page
Layout and formatting generally support readability, with clear headings and use of tables and figures
weaknesses: Required abstract is missing and sub-section numbering is inconsistent
Several tables and figures lack complete captions or units
Page numbering and in-text formatting (e.g., italics, superscripts) are erratic
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the essay adheres to basic EE structural conventions and is readable, critical presentation elements are omitted or inconsistently applied. The absence of an abstract and uneven formatting errors place this work in the 1â2 band for presentation, meriting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research questionââHow have foreign investments in Torontoâs housing market affected housing prices and affordability?ââis explicitly framed, narrow in scope and measurable through price and affordability indicators. Each analytical section returns to this question, and sub-headings (e.g., 3.1 Housing Prices, 6.1 Policies Targeting Foreign Investment) are constructed to address facets of the question directly. This clarity provides a firm anchor for discussion and avoids unnecessary divergence, satisfying the descriptor for a clearly stated, well-focused and effectively connected research question.

The student states the topic in the title and restates it clearly in the opening paragraph, immediately signalling an investigation into the impact of foreign investment on Toronto housing prices and affordability. Throughout the essay each main section (demand, supply, policy evaluation) is explicitly linked back to this central theme, demonstrating sustained focus. The scopeâisolating foreign investment as a determinantâis appropriate for an EE in Economics and allows systematic treatment through demand-and-supply analysis and policy discussion. Overall, communication of the topic and its purpose is accurate, concise and well aligned with the conventions of an Extended Essay, justifying placement in the highest band.

Data from TRREB, CMHC, Statistics Canada and government releases are used to construct tables and graphs that underpin the argument, and quotations are referenced. Nonetheless, some reliance on real-estate blogs and media articles weakens academic rigor, and several figures lack full source attribution or units. Overall, the application of sources is mostly appropriate and relevant but not consistently rigorous, meriting the 3-4 band.

The essay follows the conventional EE structure (introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion) and provides a contents page, but omits the required abstract, shows inconsistent sub-section numbering, and places limitations within the conclusion rather than as a distinct section. These omissions indicate a generally appropriate yet imperfect structure, corresponding to the 1-2 band.

Basic layout elementsâheadings, tables, and figuresâare present, and overall formatting supports readability. However, several tables/figures lack complete captions or units, page numbering is inconsistent, and in-text formatting (bold/italics, superscripts) is occasionally erratic. These shortcomings mean that while some conventions are met, others are not, justifying a placement in the 1-2 band.

The student constructs a reasoned argument linking foreign investment to price inflation and evaluates policy measures such as the Foreign Buyerâs Tax and Vacant Home Tax, acknowledging limitations and unintended consequences. Evaluation is partly critical but lacks systematic consideration of alternative explanations (e.g., interest rates, domestic speculation) and omits quantitative assessment of policy effectiveness. This balanced yet somewhat undeveloped evaluation fits the 4-6 band.

The analysis moves beyond description by linking data to shifts in supply and demand curves, considering tax incidence and noting elasticity implications. However, quantitative depth is modest: elasticity coefficients are stated but not computed, and no econometric testing is attempted. Conclusions are generally logical yet occasionally rest on assumed magnitudes. This reflects âclear analysis with mostly supported conclusionsâ, aligning with the midpoint of the 4-6 band.

The essay demonstrates a sound grasp of core economic conceptsâdemand, supply, elasticity, tax incidenceâand applies them to Torontoâs housing context with reasonable accuracy. Occasional mis-statements (e.g., equating foreign ownership rates directly with vacancy) and the absence of calculated coefficients limit coherence, yet the narrative shows more than descriptive knowledge. This places the work comfortably within the âclear though not fully coherentâ 3-4 band.

The majority of evidence comes from credible national statistics, TRREB data and government policy documents, directly aligned to the research question. Each data set is integrated into the discussion (e.g., Table comparing foreign and domestic purchases), demonstrating clear relevance. Although some less scholarly sources are used, the preponderance of appropriate, question-specific research supports placement in the 7-9 band; the limited triangulation keeps it at the lower end of that band.

The student identifies primary datasets (TRREB, Statistics Canada) and secondary materials (news outlets, academic journals) and briefly comments on potential bias, showing some informed selection. However, the methodology remains largely descriptive: periods of data collection, specific variables extracted, and the exact analytical tools (e.g., regression, elasticity calculations) are not fully detailed. Consequently, while sources are generally relevant, the completeness and explicit justification of methods are only partial, placing the work in the 3-4 band.

Economic terminologyââinelastic supplyâ, âdeadweight lossâ, âNon-Resident Speculation Taxââis generally accurate and used consistently in diagrams and explanation. Minor slips (e.g., defining supply as what producers âbuyâ) and unexplained symbols occur, preventing the precision expected at the highest band, but overall command of concepts is competent and places the work in the 3-4 range.

annotations.comment: References are presented in inconsistent formats (e.g., missing years, varying styles), undermining the professionalism and reliability of citation formatting.
Hyperlinks in the references list are inconsistently formatted and not aligned with a recognized citation style, reducing readability and uniformity.
Key statistics are drawn from blogs and news sources without academic validation, reducing the rigor and relevance of source material in the introduction.
The purpose statement identifies the topic but fails to specify measurable variables (e.g., price indices or affordability metrics), limiting methodological clarity.
This table is labelled âTable 1â again, conflicting with earlier numbering and impairing navigational clarity.
Supply is inaccurately defined as what producers âbuyâ rather than what they âsell,â indicating misunderstanding of core economic terminology.
Figure 3 shows supply and demand shifts but lacks annotation on how foreign investment versus policy measures specifically drive these movements.

The student has not included an abstract immediately following the title as required by EE conventions, which weakens the reportâs structural clarity and fails to provide a concise overview of the investigation.
The table of contents omits an entry for an abstract, disrupting the expected Extended Essay structure and making navigation less intuitive.
The research question is explicitly stated, narrow in scope and directly frames the investigation, ensuring clear focus on the impact of foreign investment on housing prices and affordability.
The definition of âforeign investorâ is too general and lacks citation of specific legal criteria or relevant statutes, weakening disciplinary precision.
The methodology header outlines parts of the approach but does not describe specific analytical tools such as regression models or elasticity calculations.
Although TRREB data bias is acknowledged, no strategy is outlined to mitigate or triangulate these biases, leaving the method under-justified.
Selection criteria for news reports are not justified and credibility is not systematically assessed, limiting methodological appropriateness.
Journals are referenced generically without naming specific publications or explaining the rationale for their selection, weakening methodological transparency.
Analysis asserts rising prices and inelastic demand but provides no computed elasticity coefficients or underlying data, limiting analytical depth.
The hypothesis linking investor purchases to immigration plans is speculative and lacks supporting evidence or references, reducing critical rigour.
Table 1 lacks explicit source attribution and units for each column, impairing the traceability and relevance of the data presented.
The claimed percentage impact of varying foreign buyer shares lacks any sensitivity analysis or confidence intervals, undermining the strength of conclusions.
Population growth narrative is descriptive but does not link demographic increases to affordability metrics or data-driven analysis.
The elasticity equation is introduced but never applied to the studentâs own data, missing an opportunity to deepen the analysis.
The narrative in this analysis is verbose; condensing discussion and directly linking each argument back to the research question would strengthen coherence and structure.
Figure 4 illustrates deadweight loss due to the tax, but the student does not quantify its magnitude or discuss policy trade-offs in terms of welfare loss.
Sub-section numbering in the contents is inconsistent (e.g., duplicate â6.1â entries), which may confuse readers about the organization of material.
Figure 1 is captioned but the axes lack units and scale descriptions, reducing the clarity of the visual presentation.
The Vacant Tax graph incorrectly shifts supply instead of demand for a tax on owners, indicating a conceptual error in economic modeling.
The conclusion summarizes findings but does not reflect on study limitations or propose future research directions, missing an evaluative component.
The limitations section acknowledges data availability but fails to analyze how these constraints materially affect the validity of the studyâs conclusions.
